publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: City in the German state of Saxony, notable for its twice-yearly trade fairs, which began in the 12th century, and for its status as a major centre of publishing from the 18th century onward.

Leipzig houses musical institutions of world importance, including the Thomaskirche (St. Thomas Church), which dates back to the 13th century and has a distinguished lineage of Kantors, including Johann Sebastian Bach (1723–50), Moritz Hauptmann (1842–68), and Karl Straube (1918–40); the Gewandhaus, the first concert hall of which was built in 1781 and subsequent buildings in 1884 and 1981, in which the Gewandhaus Orchestra, the origins of which date back to the 1740s, has had a distinguished line of music directors including Felix Mendelssohn (1835–47), Carl Reinecke (1860–95), Arthur Nikisch (1895–1922), Wilhelm Furtwängler (1922–28), and Bruno Walter (1929–33); and the Leipzig Opera, which dates back to the 18th century, with opera houses built in 1868 and 1960, for which the Gewandhaus provides the orchestra.

Two music-educational institutions standout: the Music Department of the University of Leipzig, the University dating back to the 15th century and the Department having a succession of lecturers that includes Hugo Riemann and Hermann Kretzschmar; and the Leipzig Conservatory, founded by Felix Mendelssohn in 1843. The Musikwissenschaftliches Institut (Musicological Institute) was founded in Leipzig in 1908 by Hugo Riemann.

The notable Leipzig music publishers are Breitkopf & Härtel, C. F. Peters, Friedrich Hofmeister, Ernst Eulenberg, and later Hinrichsen. Heinrich Schenker had extensive correspondence with Breitkopf & Härtel that is available on this site. That with Peters is not yet available.
From within the Schenker circle, Reinhard Oppel taught at the Leipzig Conservatory from 1927 to 1940.
